From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Black-bellied swamp snake](/w/index.php?title=Black-bellied_swamp_snake&redirect=no "Black-bellied swamp snake"))

Species of snake

| [Scientific classification](/wiki/Taxonomy_(biology) "Taxonomy (biology)") [Edit this classification](/wiki/Template:Taxonomy/Hemiaspis "Edit this classification") | |

| ***Hemiaspis signata***  ([Jan](/wiki/Giorgio_Jan "Giorgio Jan"), 1859)[[2]](#cite_note-Global_Biodiversity_Information_Facility_-_Hemiaspis_signata_(Jan,_1859)-2) | |
| [Synonyms](/wiki/Synonym_(taxonomy) "Synonym (taxonomy)") | |
| * *Alecto signata* Jan, 1859 * *Denisonia signata*  — [Boulenger](/wiki/George_Albert_Boulenger "George Albert Boulenger"), 1896 * *Drepanodontis signata*  — [Rankin](/w/index.php?title=Peter_R._Rankin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peter R. Rankin (page does not exist)"), 1972 * *Hemiaspis signata*  — [Cogger](/wiki/Harold_Cogger "Harold Cogger"), 1983 [[3]](#cite_note-3) | |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Black-bellied_Swamp_Snake_4469.jpg/250px-Black-bellied_Swamp_Snake_4469.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black-bellied_Swamp_Snake_4469.jpg)

***Hemiaspis signata*** ([common names](/wiki/Common_name "Common name"): **black-bellied swamp snake**[[4]](#cite_note-NCBI_-_March_2002_-_Two_cases_of_bites_by_the_black-bellied_swamp_snake_(Hemiaspis_signata)-4) and **marsh snake**[[5]](#cite_note-Queensland_Museum_-_2011_-_Whip_Snakes_and_Marsh_Snakes_Fact_Sheet-5)) is a [species](/wiki/Species "Species") of [venomous](/wiki/Venom "Venom") [elapid](/wiki/Elapidae "Elapidae") [snake](/wiki/Snake "Snake") [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") to [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), where it is found along the east coast.[[6]](#cite_note-Atlas_of_Living_Australia_-_Hemiaspis_signata_(Jan,_1859)_-_Black-bellied_Swamp_Snake-6)

Recognisable by two distinctive narrow white lines on the face, the colour can range from pale olive to black top with a dark grey to black belly. Adults can grow to 70 cm in length, but most specimens are smaller than this. Their diet consists mainly of skinks and frogs.[[5]](#cite_note-Queensland_Museum_-_2011_-_Whip_Snakes_and_Marsh_Snakes_Fact_Sheet-5)

It was first described in 1859 by [Giorgio Jan](/wiki/Giorgio_Jan "Giorgio Jan") as *Alecto signata*.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
